raw.notes: Current Turkish ruling family; Recep Tayyip Erdogan

raw.capital: Kahire (Mısır); Şam (Suriye); Halep; Hama; Hıms; Mayyāfāriqīn; Hısn Keyfā
raw.region_primary: Mısır, Suriye, Diyâr-ı Bekr, Cezire, Yemen
raw.regions_all: Mısır; Suriye; Filistin; Diyâr-ı Bekr; Cezire; Yemen; Hicaz
raw.narrative_en: The Ayyubids were the dynasty founded by the Kurdish-origin Saladin after abolishing the Fatimid caliphate, recapturing Jerusalem from the Crusaders at Hattin. Their confederative structure uniting Egypt and Syria symbolized the resurgence of Sunni Islam, though this decentralization eventually led to their fragmentation.

names.ja: カラオスマンオウル家

names.fr: Armée de libération du peuple de Turquie
names.es: Ejército Popular de Liberación de Turquía
names.it: Esercito di liberazione del popolo della Turchia

names.zh: 土耳其共产党/马列（新建设组织）

raw.narrative_en: The Sajids, a Turco-Iranian family from Ushrusana, rose through Abbasid service to become a significant regional power controlling Azerbaijan and Armenia. Despite their short lifespan, they represented one of the earliest examples of Turco-Islamic presence in the Caucasus. Yūsuf b. Abū al-Sāj, the dynasty's most powerful emir, played a decisive role in Abbasid politics.

raw.regions_all: Tebriz; Sultaniye; İsfahan; Bağdat; Azerbaycan; Anadolu (tâbi); Gürcistan; Horasan
raw.successor: Celâyirliler; Muzafferîler; Kurtlar; çeşitli yerel hanedanlar
raw.narrative_en: The Il-Khanids were a Mongol state centered on Iran-Iraq-Azerbaijan founded by Hülegü. Having ended the Abbasid caliphate by destroying Baghdad, the Il-Khanids underwent transformation with Ghāzān Khān's conversion to Islam. Under Öljeitü, they left magnificent architectural works in Sultaniyya, creating the most brilliant example of the Mongol-Islamic synthesis.

The Qara Qoyunlu were a Shī'ī-leaning Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Azerbaijan. A culturally brilliant period occurred under the poet-ruler Jahānshāh (pen name Ḥaqīqī). They were destroyed by Āq Qoyunlu Uzun Hasan.

raw.regions_all: Diyarbakır; Tebriz; İsfahan; Fars; Irak; Hûzistân
raw.narrative_en: The Āq Qoyunlu were a Turkmen confederation ruling in eastern Anatolia and Iran, becoming a regional power under Uzun Hasan's leadership. Though Uzun Hasan, who sought a Venetian alliance against the Ottomans, was defeated at Otlukbeli, the Āq Qoyunlu legacy directly prepared the ground for the Safavid rise.

raw.region_primary: Anadolu → Güneydoğu Avrupa → Ortadoğu → Kuzey Afrika
raw.regions_all: Söğüt/Bursa (erken); İstanbul; Anadolu; Balkanlar; Suriye; Mısır; Hicaz; Irak; Kuzey Afrika; Kırım
raw.narrative_en: The Ottomans established the longest-lasting and largest empire in Islamic history, spanning three continents over six centuries. Rising from a small Anatolian frontier principality to world power, the Ottomans became heirs to the Roman Empire through the conquest of Constantinople (1453) and the political and religious leader of the Islamic world by assuming the caliphate. Reaching the zenith of law, architecture, and military power under Süleyman the Magnificent, the empire created a distinctive civilizational model through Sinan's masterpieces, the kapıkulu system, and the multi-religious millet structure. This dynasty of 36 sultans exited the historical stage after World War I defeat through the National Struggle and the proclamation of the Republic.

raw.regions_all: Semerkand; Herât; İsfahan; Şîraz; Bağdat (geçici); Hindistan (geçici); Anadolu (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Timurids were the dynasty founded by Timur from the Barlas Turco-Mongol clan, transforming Samarkand into a world capital. Timur fundamentally redrew the political map of the Islamic world through military campaigns stretching from China to India, from Russian steppes to the Mediterranean. Yet the true Timurid legacy lies in cultural rather than military achievements: Ulugh Beg's Samarkand observatory broke new ground in astronomy, while the Herat miniature school represents the pinnacle of Islamic art. The era of Husayn Bayqara and Alī Shīr Navā'ī is the golden age of Chagatai Turkish literature. The Timurid cultural legacy was carried to India through the Mughals and to Iran through the Safavids.
The Atabegs of Yazd were a dynasty of Daylamī origin continuing the Kākūyid legacy as a small local principality in Yazd. They were taken over by the Muzaffarids during the Il-Khanid period.

The Begtiginids were a Turkic dynasty that established an atabegate in Erbil as Zangid vassals and played a significant role in the struggle against the Crusaders. Muzaffar al-Dīn Gökbörü became renowned for fighting alongside Saladin and as the greatest patron of the Mawlid celebration tradition.

The Bādūspānids ruled for approximately nine centuries in the Rūyān region of western Tabaristan, forming one of the Islamic world's longest-lasting dynasties. This mountain principality, with pre-Sasanian Iranian roots, witnessed the rise and fall of numerous empires thanks to the advantage of geographic isolation.

The Hazāraspids were a Kurdish/Lur atabegate that ruled in Greater Luristan for over 270 years. As one of the rare dynasties that survived as Il-Khanid vassals, the Hazāraspids maintained their local authority in Luristan's mountainous geography.

The Shāh-i Armanids were a Seljuq frontier principality that consolidated Turco-Islamic presence in the region by centering on the strategic city of Ahlat around Lake Van. Ahlat's position as a major trade center provided the dynasty with economic power, while continuous border conflicts with Armenian and Georgian neighbors persisted.

raw.successor: Selçuklu fethi; ardından Haçlı Edessa Kontluğu
raw.narrative_en: The Numayrids were a small Bedouin emirate ruling in the Harran and Edessa region. Related to the Mirdasids, this dynasty passed into history with the Seljuk advance and the subsequent establishment of the Crusader County of Edessa.

raw.regions_all: Diyâr-ı Bekr; Mayyāfāriqīn; Amid (Diyarbakır); Ceziretülibn Ömer
raw.narrative_en: The Marwanids were a Kurdish dynasty established in Diyar Bakr by chiefs exploiting Buyid weakness, representing the region's most important medieval emirate. By developing Silvan and Diyarbakir, they constituted one of the early examples of Kurdish political history.

raw.regions_all: Hısn Keyfâ (Hasankeyf); Âmid (Diyarbakır); Harput; Mardin; Meyyâfârikîn
raw.narrative_en: The Artuqids were an Oghuz Turkic dynasty descending from Great Seljuq frontier lords, ruling for over three centuries in three branches across southeastern Anatolia. With their rich coinage tradition and fortress building, the Artuqids pursued a strategic balancing policy in the buffer zone between Crusaders and Seljuqs.
The Ashraf (Eshref) were a very short-lived Turkic principality centered in Beyşehir. The Eşrefoğlu Mosque has survived as the principality's most lasting legacy.

The Aydın were a Turkic principality ruling in the İzmir-Aydın region, distinguished by Umur Bey's identity as a naval ghāzī. Umur Bey created an effective naval force in the Aegean against the Crusader fleet, building a vibrant cultural center also visited by Ibn Battuta.
The Beys of Alanya established a small principality in the Alanya port and traded with the Lusignan Kingdom of Cyprus.

raw.notes: Anatolian beylik of Black Sea coast

raw.narrative_en: The Chobān were a very short-lived Turkic principality centered in Kastamonu. They were overthrown by the Jāndār.

The Dulghadir were a Turkic principality centered in Maraş and Elbistan, pursuing a strategic balancing policy on the Ottoman-Mamluk border. Reaching their peak under 'Alā' al-Dawla Bozkurt Bey, this dynasty was annexed during Selim I's Egypt campaign.

raw.regions_all: Sivas; Tokat; Niksar; Malatya; Elbistan; Kayseri (geçici)
raw.narrative_en: The Dānishmendids were a Turkic principality established in north-central Anatolia after Manzikert, conducting successful defense against the Crusaders. The epic narrative formed around Dānishmend Ghāzī became one of the founding texts of Anatolian Turco-Islamic culture.

The Eretna were a dynasty of Uyghur Turkic origin derived from Il-Khanid governorship, establishing a short-lived independent state in central Anatolia centered on Sivas and Kayseri. Rising in the vacuum created by the Il-Khanid collapse, the Eretna ended with Qādī Burhān al-Dīn's coup.

The Germiyan were a Turkic principality ruling in west-central Anatolia from Kütahya, whose last lord bequeathed his territories peacefully to the Ottomans by will. Providing a cultural environment that nurtured important poets like Sheykhi, the Germiyan represented the most peaceful example of Ottoman expansion.

names.en: Hamid Oghullari and the Tekke Oghullari

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Hamid Oghullari and the Tekke Oghullari
raw.narrative_en: The Hamid were a Turkic principality ruling in two branches in southwestern Anatolia, transitioning to Ottoman control through the rare method of Murad I purchasing their territories in Islamic history.

raw.notes: Ottoman dynasty; head 2026 Harun Osman after Osman Bayezid (d.2017) -- check current head Dündar Ali Osman

raw.narrative_en: The Inanj were a small western Anatolian principality centered in Denizli. They ended with Germiyanid annexation.

raw.atlas_name_en_original: The Jāndār Oghullari or Isfandiyār Oghullari
raw.narrative_en: The Jāndār were a Turkic principality ruling for 170 years in northern Anatolia centered on Kastamonu and Sinop. The strategic control of Sinop port and copper mines provided the dynasty with economic power, and they were among the last Anatolian principalities annexed by Mehmed the Conqueror.

names.zh: 克尔克普那尔涂油摔跤节
names.fr: Le festival de lutte à l’huile de Kırkpınar

raw.narrative_en: The Mengüjekids were a Turkic principality ruling in two branches in east-central Anatolia, building the Divriği Great Mosque and Hospital listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This architectural masterpiece is one of the most original works of art from the Anatolian Seljuq period.

The Menteshe were a Turkic principality in southwestern Anatolia's Muğla region, distinguished by their naval activities and struggles against the Knights of Rhodes. Ruling for approximately 140 years, they consolidated Turco-Islamic presence on the Aegean's southern coasts.
The Parwāna were a small principality derived from the Anatolian Seljuq parwāna (high official) family, ruling briefly in the Sinop region.

names.zh: 土耳其职业足球运动员协会

raw.successor: Osmanlı Fatih Sultan Mehmed / II. Bayezid kesin ilhakı
raw.narrative_en: The Qaramanids were a Turkic principality ruling for over two centuries in central Anatolia centered on Konya, the strongest rival of the Ottomans. Claiming to be the true heirs of the Seljuq legacy, the Qaramanids continuously challenged the Ottomans and attempted to pressure them on two fronts through a Mamluk alliance. Also notable for their language consciousness through Turkish-language decrees, this dynasty ended with Mehmed the Conqueror's decisive intervention.
The Qarasi were a small Turkic dynasty that ruled briefly in the Balıkesir-Çanakkale region of western Anatolia, one of the first principalities annexed by the Ottomans.

Qādī Burhān al-Dīn represents a rare poet-sultan figure in Islamic history. Overthrowing the Eretna to establish a short-lived but culturally brilliant state in Sivas, Burhān al-Dīn left an important Dīwān in Turkish literary history. The dynasty ended when he was killed by Āq Qoyunlu Qara Yülük Osman.

The Ramadān were an Oghuz Turkic principality ruling for over two centuries in Çukurova centered on Adana. Transitioning from Mamluk to Ottoman vassalage, this dynasty maintained its longevity through a balancing policy between two great powers. They preserved semi-independent governance under the Ottomans for a long time.

raw.successor: Anadolu Selçuklu ilhakı (I. Rükneddîn Süleymân Şâh)
raw.narrative_en: The Saltuqids were a Turkic principality centered in Erzurum in northeastern Anatolia, defending the Islamic frontier against the Georgians. They maintained their strategic position at the Caucasus passes for over 130 years.

The Sarukhan were a western Anatolian Turkic principality centered in Manisa, distinguished by their naval activities. Ruling for approximately a hundred years, the Sarukhan formed one of the early examples of Turkic naval power in the Aegean.

raw.regions_all: Konya; İznik; Kayseri; Sivas; Antalya; Sinop; Erzurum
raw.narrative_en: The Seljuqs of Rūm emerged from a Great Seljuq frontier principality to become the main actor in the Turkification and Islamization of Anatolia. Over more than two centuries, they transformed Anatolia into a new geography of Islamic civilization by building caravanserais, madrasas, and mosques. Mevlānā Jalāl al-Dīn Rūmī represents the most universal intellectual legacy of this era.
The Sāhib Atā were a small principality derived from the Anatolian Seljuq vizier family, ruling briefly in Karahisar-ı Sahib.
The Tāj al-Dīn were a dynasty that established a small principality in the Niksar-Canik region of northern Anatolia. They ended with Ottoman annexation.

names.ar: أحمد بن محمد بن سلطان الحرفوش

names.zh: 土耳其人民解放党-阵线
names.fr: Parti-Front de libération des peuples de Turquie
